# Engineering Calibration

Cross-validation of calculator outputs against worked examples and published
field-data ranges from authoritative engineering references. Acts as both a
regression fence (drifting outside ±tolerance fails CI) and a defensible
cross-check for engineering reviewers.

**Companion test file:** [`src/lib/calc/calibration.test.ts`](../src/lib/calc/calibration.test.ts) — every case
in this document is encoded as a vitest assertion that runs in CI.

**Generated by:** `npx tsx scripts/calibration-values.ts` (re-run after
pipeline changes to refresh observed values).

**Last refresh:** Engine v0.4.1 — 43 calibration cases, 0 regressions outside
tolerance.

---

## How to read this document

Each case is one published reference compared against the calculator running
the appropriate scenario. The columns:

| Column | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Scenario** | Inputs over `DEFAULT_INPUTS` |
| **Source** | Authoritative engineering reference |
| **Expected** | Published value or range from the reference |
| **Observed** | What the calculator returns |
| **Δ / Verdict** | ✓ in tolerance · ⚠ within band but flagged · ✗ fail |

Tolerances vary by case — tighter for deterministic input-to-output relationships
(±2–5%), wider for derived calculations that depend on engineering assumptions
where any reasonable model could differ (±15–25%), and bracket-only for
published field-data ranges (verdict is "lands in the band").

---

## ASHRAE Handbook — HVAC Applications, Ch. 51 (Service Water Heating)

### Daily DHW demand from gpcd × occupants

**Scenario:** `DEFAULT_INPUTS` — 60-unit MF, 140 occupants (20×1.5 + 30×2.5 +
10×3.5), 20 gpcd, "medium" occupancy profile, CZ 5A Chicago.

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 51 §"Estimating Hot Water Demand", Table 7
(apartment buildings).

**Expected:** Average daily demand should be ≈ 70–100% of `occupants × gpcd =
2,800 GPD raw`. The medium-occupancy multiplier reflects that not all
occupants are home every day.

**Observed:** `avgDayDemand = 2,376 GPD → ratio 0.849`

**Δ:** 84.9% of raw — ✓ within 70–100% band

---

### Peak day:average peaking factor

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 51 Fig. 11 (residential demand profiles).

**Expected:** Peak day:average ratio for multifamily residential typically
falls in 1.0–1.5× depending on building scale and diversity.

**Observed:** `peakDayDemand / avgDayDemand = 1.242`

**Δ:** ✓ within band

---

### Peak hour ratio (residential MF)

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 51 Fig. 11; ARC supplemental analysis "Hot Water
Demand: A Detailed Look at the ASHRAE Method" (2018).

**Expected:** Peak hour ≈ 20–33% of peak-day demand for residential MF.

**Observed:** `peakHourDemand / peakDayDemand = 0.244`

**Δ:** ✓ within band

---

### Storage:peak-hour ratio

**Scenario:** Default 60-unit Chicago central condensing gas plant.

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 51 §"Storage tank sizing".

**Expected:** Storage volume = 1.0–2.5 hours of peak-hour demand for typical
MF. Lower with high recovery, upper with low recovery.

**Observed:** `1,200 gal / 720 GPH = 1.67 hr storage`

**Δ:** ✓ within band

---

### Sizing intensity (BTU/hr per occupant)

**Scenario:** Default 60-unit central gas + recirc, 90°F rise.

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 51 §"Equipment Sizing"; ASPE Vol. 2 Ch. 5.

**Expected:** 800–4,000 BTU/hr per occupant depending on storage:recovery
split (heavy storage allows lower recovery; instantaneous pushes the upper end).

**Observed:** `164,767 BTU/hr ÷ 140 occupants = 1,177 BTU/hr per occupant`

**Δ:** ✓ within band — calculator uses generous storage credit, lands at the
lower end of the published range.

---

## ASPE Data Book Vol. 2, Ch. 5 — Hunter Method (Fixture Units → GPM)

### Modified Hunter at 90 hot WSFU

**Source:** ASPE Data Book Vol. 2 Ch. 5; Roy B. Hunter, NBS Report (1940);
modified curve calibrated for low-flow fixtures (EPAct/WaterSense).

**Expected:** ~22–30 GPM in published curves.

**Observed:** `25.1 GPM`

**Δ:** ✓

---

### Modified Hunter at 200 hot WSFU

**Expected:** 38–50 GPM.

**Observed:** `46.1 GPM`

**Δ:** ✓ — calculator's piecewise √-fit is conservative (high) at the upper
end of the 100–500 WSFU branch.

---

### ⚠ Known characteristic: piecewise discontinuity at WSFU=100

The modified Hunter implementation switches branches at WSFU=100. Observed:

| WSFU | GPM |
|---|---|
| 99 | 25.9 |
| 100 | 37.0 |
| 101 | 37.1 |

The function remains monotonically non-decreasing across the join (the
calibration test guards this), but the ~11 GPM jump is a fitting artifact, not
a real Hunter-curve feature. Real-world impact is limited because most MF
buildings sit well above 100 WSFU; documenting here for transparency.

**Future improvement:** smooth the piecewise fit at the WSFU=100 boundary.

---

## Burch & Christensen 2007 — Mains Water Inlet Temperature Model

**Source:** Burch & Christensen, *Towards Development of an Algorithm for
Mains Water Temperature* (NREL/CP-550-40129, 2007).

| Climate Zone | Annual Mean (Published) | Calculator | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5A — Chicago | ~50°F | **50°F** | ✓ |
| 2A — Houston | ~70°F | **68°F** | ✓ (~3% low) |
| 5A — Chicago winter design | ~38–46°F | **43°F** | ✓ |

The annual-mean inlet derives from `CLIMATE_DESIGN[zone].avgAnnual` which is
itself calibrated to NOAA 30-yr ground-water data. Winter design applies the
seasonal curve from `monthlyInletAdjustment` (cold-climate amplitude × 0.45
for ground lag).

---

## NEEA Advanced Water Heating Specification — Central HPWH Performance

### Cold-climate central CO2 HPWH annual COP

**Scenario:** Default 60-unit Chicago central HPWH, CO2 refrigerant,
energy_star tier, mech room ambient.

**Source:** NEEA AWHS §3.2 commercial CO2 Tier 4 metering data; NREL
multifamily HPWH field studies (2020–2024).

**Expected:** 2.0–3.5 annual COP for cold-climate (CZ 5A) commercial CO2 HPWH
with standard parasitics.

**Observed:** `annualCOP = 3.31`

**Δ:** ✓ within band — at the upper end (calculator's monthly model averages
favorable mech-room temps across the year).

---

### Wastewater HP effective COP

**Source:** SHARC International technical bulletins; IEA HPT Annex 51.

**Expected:** 3.0–6.0 — wastewater HPs run higher year-round COP than
air-source because the source temperature is warm (55–65°F) and stable.

**Observed:** `wastewaterEffectiveCOP = 4.50` (default `wastewaterCOP` input).

**Δ:** ✓ within band, and `> central_hpwh annualCOP` of 3.31 as expected.

---

### Ground-loop HPWH source override (Phase G)

**Scenario:** `central_hpwh` with `hpwhSourceMode: "ground_loop"` vs
`"air_mech_room"`.

**Source:** IGSHPA Manual D § ground-source HPWH; NEEA AWHS §3.4 GSHP-
coupled commercial DHW.

**Calculator behavior:**
- Override holds `hpwhEffectiveSourceTempF = 50°F` (constant).
- In CZ 7 Duluth (mech room annual = 50°F): both modes produce
  `annualCOP ≈ 3.20` — within ±5% of each other ✓.

**⚠ Known limitation:** the current model uses an annual-average source temp,
not a monthly-seasonal one. A more granular monthly model would credit
ground-loop with winter stability (when mech room ambient drops well below the
ground loop's constant 50°F) — likely producing a 5–15% annual COP lift in
cold climates. Documented for future Phase H consideration.

---

## DOE Building America — MF Gas Central Baselines

### Central condensing gas with recirc

**Scenario:** Default 60-unit Chicago central gas + R-4 800 ft recirc loop.

**Source:** DOE Building America Multifamily Whole Building Performance
baseline tables; ENERGY STAR MFNC v1.2 reference designs.

**Expected:** 80–200 therms/unit/yr depending on occupancy density, recirc
insulation, and climate.

**Observed:** `7,105 therms / 60 units = 118.4 therms/unit/yr`

**Δ:** ✓ — middle of the published band.

---

### Atmospheric vs condensing gas use ratio

**Scenario:** Same plant, atmospheric (UEF 0.78) vs condensing (UEF 0.95).

**Source:** ASHRAE 90.1-2022 §6 minimum boiler efficiencies; AHRI 1500.

**Expected:** Theoretical ratio = 0.95 / 0.78 = 1.218 (1.10–1.30 with
ancillary losses).

**Observed:** `Atmospheric / Condensing = 1.218`

**Δ:** ✓ exact match — efficiency-only model with no ancillary penalties.

---

## ENERGY STAR MFNC v1.2 — In-Unit Baselines

| System | Per-Unit Annual | Expected Range | Observed | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| In-unit gas tank (UEF 0.80) | therms/unit/yr | 50–200 (default occupancy) | **118.4** | ✓ |
| In-unit HPWH (Tier 4) | kWh/unit/yr | 800–2,000 | **1,056** | ✓ |
| In-unit resistance | kWh/unit/yr | 2,000–4,000 | **3,297** | ✓ |

**Sources:**
- ENERGY STAR MFNC v1.2 reference designs
- AHRI Directory 1300 (residential)
- NEEA HPWH Field Studies
- ACEEE multifamily field data

**Occupancy-sensitive in-unit modeling (engine v0.4.1):** all per-unit and
monthly in-unit / combi DHW calcs now consume `avgDayDemand / totalUnits`
rather than the static ASHRAE_APT_DEMAND tier value. This makes per-unit
energy properly occupancy-aware when the user picks `demandMethod:
"occupancy"` (linear scaling with `gpcd × occupantsPerUnit`) or
`demandMethod: "hunter"` (fixture-mix-derived). Default `demandMethod:
"ashrae"` is mathematically unchanged because `avgDayDemand / totalUnits`
reduces to `ASHRAE_APT_DEMAND[occupancyProfile].avg` by construction —
preserving every legacy baseline.

**Per-bedroom differentiation (combi only):** when method=occupancy, the
combi DHW load splits per-bedroom-count by occupant share — a 3BR with 3.5
default occupants consumes ~3.5× the studio's 1.0-occupant load. Falls back
to building-uniform per-unit under ashrae and hunter methods (no
per-bedroom occupant data in those methods).

---

## ASHRAE 90.1-2022 — Recirculation Loop Standby Loss + Control Modes

### Continuous-pumping baseline

**Scenario:** Default 800-ft recirc loop, R-4 insulation, 125°F return,
70°F ambient pipe.

**Source:** ASHRAE 90.1-2022 §6.5.5 commentary; ASPE Vol. 2 Ch. 5 worked
recirc-loss examples.

**Expected:** 1,500–4,000 BTU/hr for typical MF loop with R-4 insulation.

**Observed:** `recircLossRawBTUH = 2,832 BTU/hr`

**Δ:** ✓ — middle of band.

---

### Demand-controlled recirc reduction

**Source:** ACEEE multifamily recirculation studies; ASHRAE 90.1-2022 §6.5.5
exception (b).

**Expected:** 60–80% loss reduction vs continuous.

**Observed:** `1 − (demand / continuous) = 0.700` (70% reduction)

**Δ:** ✓

---

### Time-clock recirc (16 hr/day) reduction

**Source:** ASHRAE 90.1-2022 §6.5.5; ACEEE distribution studies.

**Expected:** 35–60% reduction (lower than pure pro-rata 16/24 = 33% off
because schedules typically exclude overnight hours).

**Observed:** `1 − (timeclock / continuous) = 0.500` (50% reduction)

**Δ:** ✓ — calculator uses `(hours/24) × 0.75` formula → 16/24 × 0.75 = 0.50.

---

## EPA eGRID 2022 — Grid Carbon Intensity

| Source | Published Factor | Observed Factor | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| MROW (Upper Midwest grid) | ~0.85–1.10 lb CO2e/kWh | **1.040 lb/kWh** | ✓ |
| Natural gas combustion | 11.7 lb CO2/therm | **11.700 lb/therm** | ✓ exact |

Source: EPA eGRID 2022 published values; EPA 2024 GHG emission factors hub
for fuels.

## ASHRAE Apps Ch. 35 — Solar Thermal Water Heating

### 600 sqft active solar in CZ 4A NYC

**Scenario:** 60-unit central gas + 600 sqft collectors + 400 gal solar
storage tank + DWHR off.

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 35 (Solar Energy Use); ASHRAE 93 collector test
method; CSI / Title 24 calibration cases.

**Expected:** 0.20–0.70 annual solar fraction for moderately oversized
collector array in mixed climate.

**Observed:** `annualSolarFraction = 0.273`

**Δ:** ✓ within band — lower end (NYC is mid-latitude with significant
winter cloud cover).

---

### Solar fraction cap

**Source:** Practical limit at which excess collector capacity is shed during
peak months (real systems do not approach 100% annual fraction without
dump loads).

**Expected:** ≤ 0.85 annual fraction even with extreme oversizing.

**Observed:** `2,000 sqft solar in CZ 2A Houston` → `annualSolarFraction =
0.85` (capped) ✓

---

## CSA B55.1 / B55.2 — Drainwater Heat Recovery

### DWHR effective inlet lift

**Scenario:** 50% effective DWHR × 50% coverage on default scenario.

**Source:** CSA B55.2 effectiveness test method; NREL DWHR field studies.

**Expected:** 3–15°F annual-average inlet lift (typical shower-event ΔT
~50–60°F × 0.5 effectiveness × 0.5 coverage = 12.5–15°F max).

**Observed:** `annualDwhrLiftF = 11.25°F`

**Δ:** ✓ within band.

---

## Phase A–G Architectural Effects

### Cascade boilers — efficiency bonus

**Scenario:** Default plant, single boiler vs 4-boiler cascade.

**Source:** AERCO / Lochinvar / Patterson-Kelley cascade application
literature; AHRI 1500 part-load efficiency curves.

**Expected:** +1% per boiler capped at +3% effective efficiency bonus →
1–4% gas reduction.

**Observed:** `1 − (cascade4 / single) = 3.06%` reduction

**Δ:** ✓ — at the cap, as expected for 4 boilers (3% cap on bonus).

---

### N+1 redundancy — capex gross-up

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 51 §"Equipment Redundancy"; ASPE Vol. 2 Ch. 5.

**Expected:** N+1 raises installed cost vs N (no redundancy) for the same
duty.

**Observed:** N+1 capex > N capex ✓ — directional check passes.

---

### CHP DHW heat-recovery coverage

**Scenario:** 35 kW CHP, H/P ratio 1.7, 7,000 hr/yr, 80% utilization.

**Calculation:** `recoverable_BTU = 35 kW × 3,412 × 1.7 × 7,000 × 0.80 =
1,138 MMBTU/yr` vs default annual DHW load ≈ 766 MMBTU/yr. CHP exceeds DHW
load → coverage saturates at 100%.

**Observed:** `chpCoverageFraction = 1.000` ✓

**75 kW CHP electric generation:** `75 × 7,000 = 525,000 kWh/yr` ✓ exact
(informational only — not on the DHW account).

---

### HRC — heat-recovery chiller

**Scenario:** Default plant + 50 tons cooling, 60% year-round, COP 4.0.

**Source:** ASHRAE Apps Ch. 35 §"Heat Recovery from Refrigeration"; ASPE
Vol. 2 Ch. 9.

**Expected:** 15–100% gas reduction depending on cooling-load match.

**Observed:**
- Default 50-ton plant: 100% reduction (oversized for 60-unit DHW load) ✓
- Reduced 5-ton, 30% year-round plant: 21.8% coverage ✓

---

### Per-floor decentralized HPWH — recirc loss

**Source:** ACEEE multifamily distribution studies; per-floor design saves
recirc length per zone.

**Expected:** Per-floor (4 zones) recirc loss < central HPWH (single loop).

**Observed:** `708 BTU/hr` per-floor vs `2,832 BTU/hr` central → 75% reduction.

**Δ:** ✓ directional, in line with `1/N` loop-length scaling for N=4 zones.

---

## Cross-System Engineering Sanity

| Check | Expected | Observed | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Central resistance kWh / Central HPWH kWh | 2.5–4.5× (≈ COP) | **3.12×** | ✓ |
| HRC gas reduction vs central gas (default 50 tons) | 15–100% | **100%** | ✓ |
| Per-floor recirc loss < central HPWH recirc loss | True | True | ✓ |
| `runCalc` is deterministic across repeated calls | True | True | ✓ |

---

## Summary

**Total cases:** 43 calibration assertions across 11 reference sources.

**Verdict:** All within tolerance. No cases produce values outside the
published reference bands at default scenarios.

**Caveats / known model simplifications:**

1. **Hunter curve discontinuity at WSFU=100** — piecewise √-fit jumps ~11 GPM.
   Monotonic, conservative; smoothing is a candidate for future refinement.
2. **HPWH source mode uses annual-average temps** — no monthly seasonality.
   Under-credits ground-loop benefit in cold climates where mech room ambient
   drops well below the ground's stable 50°F. Phase H candidate.
3. **CHP recovery is bounded only by load and runtime** — no temperature-
   match check between CHP exhaust and DHW setpoint. Conservative on the
   cost side (under-credits practical thermal mismatch), but recovery
   utilization factor of 0.80 partially absorbs the gap.

**Resolved in v0.4.1:** in-unit gas tank model is now occupancy-sensitive
under `demandMethod: "occupancy"`. Per-unit therms scales linearly with
`gpcd × occupantsPerUnit`; combi DHW splits per-bedroom by occupant share.
Default ashrae method preserved exactly. New calibration cases:
- *In-unit gas tank: per-unit therms scales linearly with gpcd (occupancy method)*
- *In-unit gas tank: monthly energy scales with gpcd (occupancy method)*
- *Combi DHW: 3BR per-unit annual ≈ 3.5× studio per-unit (occupancy method)*

**Re-validate after:** any change to `pipeline.ts`, `auto-size.ts`, or core
constants in `engineering/constants.ts`. The vitest suite runs all 40 cases
on every CI build.

---

## Reference list

1. ASHRAE Handbook — HVAC Applications, Ch. 51 (Service Water Heating), 2023
2. ASHRAE Handbook — HVAC Applications, Ch. 35 (Solar Energy Use), 2023
3. ASHRAE 90.1-2022 §6.5.5 (Service Water Heating Recirculation)
4. ASHRAE 93 (Solar Collector Test Method)
5. ASPE Data Book Vol. 2, Ch. 5 (Service Water Heating)
6. AHRI Directory 1300 (Residential Water Heaters), Directory 1500 (Boilers),
   Directory 1700 (Commercial HPWH)
7. NEEA Advanced Water Heating Specification (AWHS) §3.2 / §3.4
8. NYSERDA Multifamily HPWH Guidance Document (2022–2024)
9. NREL/CP-550-40129 — Burch & Christensen, "Towards Development of an
   Algorithm for Mains Water Temperature" (2007)
10. NREL Multifamily HPWH Field Studies (2020–2024)
11. EPA eGRID 2022 (grid emission factors)
12. EPA 2024 GHG Emission Factors Hub (fuel combustion)
13. ENERGY STAR Multifamily New Construction (MFNC) v1.2 reference designs
14. ACEEE Multifamily Hot Water Distribution / Recirculation Studies
15. CSA B55.1 / B55.2 — Drainwater Heat Recovery test + installation
16. Roy B. Hunter, NBS Building Materials Structures Report 65 (1940)
17. SHARC International Technical Bulletins (wastewater HP)
18. IEA HPT Annex 51 (Wastewater Heat Recovery)
19. IGSHPA Manual D — Ground-Source HPWH applications
20. DOE Building America Multifamily Performance baseline tables
21. AERCO / Lochinvar / Patterson-Kelley cascade application literature
22. ICF CHP Database (cogeneration system specifications)
23. ARC Engineering — "Hot Water Demand: A Detailed Look at the ASHRAE
    Method" (2018)
